title: Some snakes can fly — and they're better at gliding than flying squirrels.
short: Paradise tree snakes in Southeast Asia launch themselves from trees and flatten their bodies to glide up to 24 meters through the air. No wings, no legs, no problem.
twist: Snakes weren't scary enough on the ground. Now they fly.
more:
Chrysopelea snakes achieve flight by launching themselves from tree branches, flattening their ribs to double their body width, and sucking in their stomachs to create a concave cross-section on their underside. This effectively turns their entire body into a long, flexible wing. They then undulate through the air in an S-shape, which research has shown actually generates lift — not just drag reduction.

High-speed camera studies by Virginia Tech physicist Jake Socha revealed that the aerial undulation is critical to flight stability. Without it, the snakes tumble. The S-shaped movement distributes lift along the body and creates a dynamic equilibrium that allows controlled gliding over distances up to 24 meters — further than many flying squirrels can manage. They can even execute mid-air turns to navigate around obstacles.

The US Department of Defense has actually funded research into these snakes' flight mechanics, hoping to apply the principles to aerial robot design. A flexible, wingless drone that could launch from any surface and glide with precision would have obvious applications. It's one of nature's most improbable engineering solutions: a creature with no legs, no wings, and no fins that somehow became one of the best gliders in the animal kingdom.

title: Ancient Romans used urine as mouthwash — and it actually worked.
short: Roman dentistry included rinsing with human urine. The ammonia in urine is an effective cleaning agent that kills bacteria. Urine-based mouthwash was used well into the 18th century.
twist: Ancient dental hygiene: disgusting, but scientifically sound.
more:
Ammonia, which forms as urine decomposes, is a genuinely powerful antibacterial and whitening agent. The chemistry is sound — ammonia breaks down bacterial cell walls and can dissolve organic stains, including tooth discoloration. Roman poet Catullus mocked a Spaniard for having white teeth, implying he must have been using urine. It was so mainstream that it was barely considered unusual.

The Romans didn't just use local supply — they imported urine from Portugal, which was considered premium quality (the reasons are lost to history, but diet likely played a role). Emperor Vespasian famously taxed the urine trade, leading to the Latin proverb 'pecunia non olet' — money doesn't stink. Public urine collection vessels were placed on streets, and fullers (cloth workers) used the collected urine to clean and process wool and textiles.

The practice survived far longer than most people realize. Urine-based mouthwash was used in parts of Europe well into the 18th century. In some cultures, it persisted even longer — morning urine was used as a folk remedy for toothaches and gum disease in rural communities into the 1800s. Modern dentistry only replaced it when Listerine was introduced in the 1880s, originally as a surgical antiseptic. The gap between 'disgusting' and 'scientifically effective' is smaller than our squeamishness wants to admit.

title: The observable universe has a smell — and it's apparently like rum and raspberries.
short: The chemical ethyl formate, found in vast dust clouds at the center of the Milky Way, smells like rum and tastes like raspberries. Space has a flavor profile.
twist: The galaxy smells like a cocktail. The universe is a party.
more:
In 2009, astronomers at the IRAM telescope in Spain detected ethyl formate in Sagittarius B2 — a massive molecular cloud near the center of our galaxy, about 26,000 light-years from Earth. This particular ester is the same chemical compound responsible for giving raspberries their distinctive flavor and rum its characteristic aroma. The discovery was part of a broader search for amino acids in space.

Sagittarius B2 is one of the largest molecular clouds in the Milky Way, containing enough alcohol (ethanol and methanol) to fill 400 trillion trillion pints of beer. It's a cosmic chemistry lab where complex organic molecules form on the surface of dust grains, shielded from destructive ultraviolet radiation. Over 200 different molecules have been identified in interstellar space, including sugars, alcohols, and the building blocks of amino acids.

Astronauts have also reported that space itself has a smell — after spacewalks, they describe a lingering odor on their suits that resembles seared steak, gunpowder, and raspberries. Scientists believe this smell comes from polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons — complex carbon molecules produced by dying stars. So the universe doesn't just smell like raspberries in one spot. The scent of space is woven into the chemistry of stellar death itself.

title: There are more viruses on Earth than stars in the entire observable universe.
short: Scientists estimate there are 10³¹ (10 nonillion) viruses on Earth. The observable universe contains roughly 10²⁴ stars. Viruses outnumber stars by a factor of 10 million.
twist: You're not surrounded by viruses. You're swimming in them.
more:
If you lined up all the viruses on Earth end to end, they'd stretch about 100 million light-years — enough to span multiple galaxy clusters. Laid side by side, their combined mass would outweigh all the elephants on Earth by a factor of a thousand. And yet each individual virus is so small that it can only be seen with an electron microscope.

The vast majority of these viruses — roughly 97% — are bacteriophages, viruses that exclusively infect bacteria. They're harmless to humans and are actually essential to life on Earth. In the ocean alone, phages kill approximately 20% of all bacterial biomass every single day. This viral carnage releases nutrients back into the water, fueling the base of the marine food chain. Without viruses, ocean ecosystems would likely collapse.

Viruses have also been one of the most powerful engines of evolution. Through a process called horizontal gene transfer, viruses move genetic material between organisms — including across species boundaries. An estimated 8% of the human genome consists of ancient viral DNA that was inserted into our ancestors' genomes millions of years ago. Some of this viral DNA now serves critical functions: the gene that allows the placenta to form during pregnancy was originally delivered by a virus. We are, in a very real sense, partly made of ancient viruses.
